senses.definition: used to indicate that one is not giving all the details of what happened but only the main points

examples.x: Long story short, I missed my flight because I overslept.
Long story short, the car broke down in the middle of nowhere, and we had to wait for hours for help to arrive.
Long story short, the argument escalated, and they ended up breaking up.
Long story short, the team won the championship in the final seconds of the game.

senses.definition: to ask some questions in order to figure out what someone is thinking of or what their opinion about a particular person or thing is

examples.x: The company was putting out feelers about the possibility of relocating to a new city.
The politician was putting out feelers to see if there was any support for his tax reform plan.
The unemployed man was putting out feelers to see if any of his former colleagues had any job openings.
The couple was putting out feelers to see if any of their friends would be interested in adopting their dog.

word_list.pronunciation: ðə ˈʌðɚ flˈɪp ˈɑːpəsˌɪt sˈaɪd ʌvðə kˈɔɪn

senses.definition: a different way of thinking or approaching a situation

examples.x: While exercise can be beneficial for physical health, the flip side of the coin is that it can also lead to injuries if done improperly.
Technology has revolutionized communication and access to information, but the opposite side of the coin is that it has also increased our vulnerability to cyberattacks and privacy concerns.

Living in a city offers many advantages, such as access to diverse cultures and entertainment options, but the flip side of the coin is that it can also be more expensive and crowded than living in a rural area.

senses.definition: said before using a famous, overused, or made-up phrase

examples.x: The politician, in an attempt to emphasize his point, declared, "We must, to coin a phrase, think outside the box for innovative solutions."
After the disastrous presentation, she sighed and said, "Well, that didn't go as planned – to coin a phrase – it was a train wreck."
Trying to explain his unconventional theory, the scientist stated, "In our research, we are, to coin a phrase, exploring uncharted waters in the world of physics."
The comedian, poking fun at his own awkwardness, remarked, "I always say, to coin a phrase, I have a face made for radio."
Reflecting on the unexpected turn of events, she noted, "Life is, to coin a phrase, a roller coaster with its ups and downs."

senses.definition: to be brave or sociable enough to deliver a lengthy public speech without allowing any interruption

examples.x: During the debate, the senator held the floor for an extended period, passionately arguing for the proposed legislation.
In the team meeting, the project manager held the floor to provide updates on the current status and address concerns.
The professor held the floor, captivating the audience with a compelling lecture on the history of the ancient civilization.
As the spokesperson for the group, she held the floor and presented the case for increased workplace diversity.
During the town hall meeting, community members were given the opportunity to hold the floor and share their perspectives on the proposed changes to local zoning laws.

senses.definition: used to imagine how things would be if the situation was different

examples.x: I’m sure they’re safe, or else we’d have heard something.
They can't want it, or else they'd request it.
We must be there by six, or else we'll miss the beginning.
You know I really care about you, or else I wouldn’t be here.

senses.definition: used to emphasize that the exact opposite of what is said is true

examples.x: Usually, people finish high school and then go to college, but for him, it's the other way around—he's attending college first and then completing high school.
Traditionally, the older sibling mentors the younger one, but in their case, it's the other way around; the younger sister is guiding her older brother through his career choices.
Normally, you'd find a job and then pursue further education, but she decided to do it the other way around, completing her master's degree before entering the workforce.
In most cases, the teacher assigns homework and the students complete it, but in this unique classroom, it's the other way around—the students propose projects, and the teacher provides guidance.
Typically, people buy a house and then acquire furniture, but in their case, it's the other way around; they furnished their home before finalizing the purchase.

senses.definition: a situation in which two parties exchange ideas in order to find the best solution or approach to something

examples.x: In business negotiations, there is often a need for give and take to reach an agreement that satisfies both parties' interests.
The team's success was attributed to the give and take among its members, who shared ideas and collaborated effectively.
The political debate showcased a healthy give and take of ideas, as candidates discussed various perspectives to address the issues at hand.

senses.definition: to manage to state one's opinions regarding something after or in the middle of someone else's prolonged speech

examples.x: Every time we meet for coffee, she talks so much that it's hard to get a word in edgewise.
During the heated debate, I struggled to get a word in edgewise as everyone was expressing their opinions passionately.
In the lively family reunion, it was challenging for the quiet cousin to get a word in edgewise among the boisterous relatives.
The interview panel was so engrossed in their discussion that the candidate found it difficult to get a word in edgewise to highlight her qualifications.
At the crowded networking event, I tried to share my experiences with others, but it felt impossible to get a word in edgewise with the constant buzz of conversation.

senses.definition: a difficult or controversial issue or topic that is uncomfortable or risky to handle

examples.x: The scandal surrounding the company's financial practices became a hot potato that no executive wanted to address directly.
The controversial policy decision turned into a hot potato as various political figures distanced themselves from it.
When the team realized the magnitude of the project's challenges, it became a hot potato, with everyone trying to shift responsibility to someone else.
The budget deficit issue has become a hot potato in the government, as officials are reluctant to propose unpopular measures to address it.
The employee's misconduct allegation turned into a hot potato for the HR department, requiring careful handling to avoid legal repercussions.

senses.definition: used before stating a fact that one wants someone to know or remember

examples.x: Before we proceed, I'd like to state, for the record, that I was not involved in the decision-making process.
The spokesperson clarified, for the record, that the company is committed to environmental sustainability and has implemented various green initiatives.
For the record, I want to emphasize that my absence from the meeting was due to unforeseen circumstances and not a lack of commitment.
The athlete, during the press conference, asserted, for the record, that the recent performance was affected by an injury and not a lack of effort.
Let me, for the record, make it clear that the research findings were based on extensive data analysis and rigorous methodology.

senses.definition: a subject about which there are different opinions or disagreements

examples.x: Whether we should have used a different approach in the last project is a moot point now; we need to focus on the upcoming one.
Discussing the merits of the old policy versus the new one is a moot point since the company has already implemented the changes.
Whether he would have won the election is a moot point; the results are final, and we need to move forward.
Debating the ethics of the decision is a moot point at this stage; what's crucial is how we rectify the situation.
Arguing about who is responsible for the mistake is a moot point; our priority is fixing it and preventing similar issues in the future.

word_list.pronunciation: bɹˈɔːdən ɛkspˈænd wˈaɪdən wˈʌnz hɚɹˈaɪzən mˈaɪnd

senses.definition: to expand one's knowledge, experiences, or perspectives, often by exploring new places, ideas, or cultures, with the aim of gaining a broader understanding of the world

examples.x: Traveling to different countries can broaden your horizons by exposing you to new cultures, traditions, and ways of life.
Reading books from various genres and authors is an excellent way to expand your mind and broaden your literary horizons.
Joining a debate club or attending public lectures can widen your horizons by exposing you to different viewpoints and arguments.
Taking up a new hobby or learning a new skill can broaden your mind and provide fresh perspectives on life.
Engaging in meaningful conversations with people from diverse backgrounds can expand your mind and widen your understanding of the world.

senses.definition: a state when a person gets too focused on one thing, which can cause them to miss other important things

examples.x: The manager's tunnel vision towards cost-cutting measures led to overlooking the long-term consequences on employee morale.
The politician's tunnel vision on a single policy issue prevented them from addressing the broader concerns of their constituents.
The detective's tunnel vision on a particular suspect hindered the investigation, as alternative leads were ignored.
The artist's tunnel vision on perfectionism caused them to overlook the beauty and value of imperfections in their work.
The student's tunnel vision on achieving the highest grades caused them to disregard the importance of personal growth and exploration of other subjects.

senses.definition: in a way that makes someone look bad or incapable

examples.x: The company's unethical practices were exposed, putting them in a bad light in the eyes of consumers.
The controversial remarks by the celebrity were taken out of context and presented in a bad light.
The media portrayed the politician in a bad light, focusing only on their past mistakes.
The misunderstanding between the two friends was blown out of proportion, painting their friendship in a bad light.

senses.definition: in a way that highlights someone's positive qualities or capabilities

examples.x: The author's latest book received critical acclaim, showcasing their storytelling abilities in a good light.
The candidate's strong qualifications and experience were presented in a good light during the job interview.
The charity's efforts to help the community were recognized, putting them in a good light among donors.
The school's dedication to providing quality education and fostering a supportive learning environment was showcased in a good light by satisfied parents.
The teacher's positive reinforcement and encouragement helped the students view themselves in a good light, boosting their confidence and motivation.

senses.definition: used to express one's opinions or beliefs

examples.x: For my money, this restaurant serves the best pizza in town.
In terms of action movies, for my money, the classic ones are still the most exciting.
When it comes to fashion, for my money, comfort should always come first.
For my money, this brand offers the most reliable and durable electronic devices.
For my money, this book is the most thought-provoking and well-written piece of literature I've read in years.

senses.definition: a temporary state of feeling that causes one to have a particular set of emotions, beliefs, and behaviors toward a particular person or thing

examples.x: I can't discuss the issue with him right now; he's not in the right frame of mind.
To tackle this problem effectively, you need to be in a positive frame of mind.
Being in a creative frame of mind, she came up with innovative solutions to the challenges.
It's difficult to make decisions when you're in a stressed frame of mind, so take a moment to relax.
He approached the task with a determined frame of mind, determined to overcome any obstacles.

senses.definition: according to one’s opinion

examples.x: In her eyes, the world was a place of endless possibilities.
In his eyes, she was the most beautiful woman he had ever seen.
In their eyes, the old man was a wise and respected member of the community.

senses.definition: to have the same thoughts, ideas, opinions, etc. about something

examples.x: Before the meeting, let's make sure we are all reading from the same page about the project goals.
It's crucial that the team members read from the same page when presenting the proposal to the client.
The success of the band depends on each member reading from the same page during rehearsals.
In negotiations, it's essential that both parties read from the same page to reach a mutually beneficial agreement.
The project manager emphasized the need for the entire team to read from the same page to maintain consistency in their work.

senses.definition: to express the same opinions or have the same understanding as someone else

examples.x: The married couple always know how to sing from the same hymn book, even when they're arguing.

senses.definition: used to summarize or describe something briefly

examples.x: The movie, in a nutshell, is about a group of friends who embark on an adventurous journey.
In a nutshell, the company's new strategy focuses on expanding into emerging markets.
His argument, in a nutshell, is that technology has improved communication but reduced face-to-face interaction.
In a nutshell, the project aims to improve efficiency and reduce costs.
In a nutshell, the training program provides employees with essential skills and knowledge.
The project, in a nutshell, aims to increase efficiency by streamlining processes and reducing costs.
